speaker: interviewer
text: September 25, 1996. We are conducting an interview with the survivor Mr. Paul Kuttner. Mr. Kuttner, you find yourself now in Bryanston College. And you hardly speak the language, and you are taken to the classes. And now you have all these many subjects. Tell us about your adjustment to the school and how quickly were you able to work within the English language and with the language.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at first, as you can imagine, it was very, very difficult. Even though I had a basic knowledge of English, it really didn't help me very much at first at least because the teachers had their own different accents which were intelligible maybe to the rest of the school but not to me, including the horrible language action-- accent. But, and we even had one cockney teacher. So it took me a few-- I would say about a couple more months before I could really get adjusted to all of these different accents and begin to grasp what they were teaching us. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the life in school was-- after I'd really gotten used to everything-- it was sheer heaven. I loved every second of it. Not only because I could start learning again, but all the students, except one of them, were wonderful in every respect. They tried to be my friends. They helped me whenever they could or when I didn't understand something or lost my way on the huge grounds there. They helped me find my way around. Except this one boy-- I cannot remember, very tall boy-- was actually a Nazi and a great anti-Semite. And he often made anti-Jewish remarks right in front of me. And then he was actually talked down by some of the other students telling him to shut up or mind your own business or get out of here. But otherwise, I say it was quite wonderful. It was more like a country life because it was right in Dorset in the country. And as I mentioned before, it was a Duke-- a former Duke's home, a huge place of about, I would say, between 100 and 150 rooms, which were later converted into classrooms and dormitories. And the grounds themselves-- we played tennis, and we had our rowing. I was a member of what they call the 'Eight,' later, not immediately. But I was a great sports fan at the time, and I am just the opposite. I'm no longer interested in sports. But at the time, of course when you are young and you're 16, 17 years old, everything is physical. And it was sheer heaven. It was more like a luxury-- luxury hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in school-- well, my first semester-- I should point this out. My first semester actually ended sometime in June of 1939 when there was the summer break. Now, I had no way of knowing what I was supposed to do on my summer break.

speaker: interviewee
text: I waited. The entire school, everybody, was picked up or left on their own. And I was left in this huge building all by myself. And I went to Dr. Joad, the director, the headmaster, and asked him what am I supposed to do? He said somebody will pick you up shortly. So finally, I took my little suitcase, weighing 25 pounds still, with me. I waited outside. And about an hour or two later, a Rolls Royce arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I did not think for one minute that this was meant for me. But a chauffeur got out, knocked at the door. Everything was locked already by that time. And I asked him who he wanted to see. And he said-- he looked at a piece of paper and said, somebody by the name of Paul Kuttner.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, that's me. He said, OK, hop in. So I said, are you sure this is for me? And he said, yes, absolutely. And so I asked him, are you my sponsor? He said, oh, no, I'm just the chauffeur. I'm going to take you to your sponsor. So finally, we rode down all the way from Dorset to Woking, Surrey near London, which is a suburb of London-- very exclusive subway. And after about four hours-- You don't mean 'subway.' You meant to say 'suburb?' Did you want to say, it was an exclusive--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sorry--

speaker: interviewee
text: Woking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, suburb. I'm so sorry. Yes, suburb.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, we got there. And I remember these huge amounts of roses in front-- yellow and red roses-- in front of a large villa. And I asked if this is the place where I'm going to stay? And he said, yes. And then I asked him who am I supposed to stay with? And he said, you will find out. Just wait inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I waited inside. And stood there in a beautiful drawing room, here they call it living room. And looked around and waited and waited. And after about 10, 15, 20 minutes, an elderly lady appeared-- in her 60s or early 70s. And she went to me, and I have no idea-- she embraced me right away and said she's so glad to meet me. And she introduced herself as Lady Pulbrook.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, yes, my Lady, I don't even know why I'm here. She said, well, you are going to spend your summer vacation here. So I said, are you my sponsor? And she said, no, actually, my husband is. And you will meet him in a few minutes. And he came a few minutes later-- a very nice, huge man, about 6' 6' or so-- and shook my hands. And I asked him what his job was because I had no idea. I mean, I just said, what do you do for a living? And he said, did you ever hear of Lloyd's of London? And I said, sure, everybody-- I even saw a movie with Tyrone Power called He said, well, I'm the chairman. I said, well, that's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those were my sponsors. They were financially responsible for me. And throughout my stay in England, I could always live with them, and they were my friends. And they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sir Eustace Pulbrook and Lady Pulbrook. I can't remember her first name. But I always had to call him Sir Eustace. And once in awhile, I called him Doody because I noticed his family called him Doody, whatever it may mean. And I called him Doody, but he didn't seem to take offense.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I stayed all summer. First, we stayed for a week or so in Woking in his palatial home. And I'll never forget the first time I woke up there in the morning. Somebody knocked at my door, and I said probably now I have to get up. And finally, the door opened and a butler came in with a tray of tea and put it on my bed. And I said, that's the way the rich live. There were a few cookies there, and that was that. And then we had breakfast. And I stayed there for a week. And I played tennis and-- with their son Roger Pulbrook. And then finally, we moved down to a vacation resort in Devonshire, which is on the south coast, and-- in a palatial home. Just wonderful. Those were the-- my summer vacation, the last vacation before World War II broke out in September. In fact, on September 1, we returned to Woking, Surrey, and read in the paper that Hitler had invaded Poland. Then two days later, we listened at 10 o'clock in the morning or 11 o'clock in the morning Chamberlain declaring war on Hitler, on Nazi Germany because Hitler had not withdrawn his troops from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all that time, from the time you left your mother in February till this September, did you have any communication from your mother, your father, your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely, yes. Not my father so much because he couldn't write anymore because of his stroke. But my sister visited him and wrote to me about him. And my mother and I were constantly in touch by mail. Unfortunately-- and I never forgive myself for it-- is every time I answered her letter I threw it away. Of course today, it would be the most priceless things in my life. But I didn't realize, of course at the time, that war would break out, and that was that. And we did correspond until, I think, the end of August. And then of course, everything was cut off because Germany-- or England declared war on Germany, and that was the end of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the reaction of Sir Eustace or people around him when-- when England declared war on Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they gathered already that it looked very, very dangerous with Hitler invading all these different countries-- Austria and the Sudetenland and Danzig. And after Danzig, I think, Chamberlain had already threatened Hitler with war if he invaded another country. And, of course, he did. And in September when he moved into Poland-- and that was the end of it because Poland had never been German. So they declared war. But we didn't realize it-- it actually would occur. We just knew there was a danger. It might occur. And so did the English. But they were totally unprepared as you know. There are these famous pictures of-- of farmers waiting with pitchforks with which they were fighting-- were going to fight the Nazi invaders. They didn't even have bullets or any ammunition for their guns, the British Home Guards. It was a catastrophe.

speaker: interviewer
text: But since you were in a very exceptional, upper middle class aristocratic British home--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm asking you, do you recall their reaction when the war was declared? Do you recall any discussions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, they-- they-- they expected that sooner or later would. And when the blow finally came, they thought, oh, it probably would be over very soon. And then what they called 'the phony war,' it started in September, '39. They call it the phone war because between September, '39 and April or March of 1940, there was really nothing. And the entire family, the Pulbrook family thought, well, it would simply fizzle out. Nothing is going to happen. And after a time, peace would be declared again. But then, of course, it started when Hitler invaded Norway and Denmark and later.

speaker: interviewer
text: While-- while you were spending the summer with the Pulbrooks and later on when September came, two things-- did you have a very interesting social life? Were you a part of their social life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- and did you meet some special people? And the second question is in September, don't the schools begin in England? Did you go back to Bryanston again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So please tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as the summer vacation were concerned, it was heaven. And I was introduced to quite a number of people, guests of Sir Eustace. And one of these guests was his best friend, a British actor by the name of Leslie Howard. And I became very friendly with Leslie because he had made a movie called about the Nazi period. And he had already made that in about 1938 as far as I remember. And so he was very much interested in the Hitler period and certainly my experiences in Nazi Germany. And we became quite friendly. I never visited him, but I saw him frequently in-- at the Pulbrooks. And once in a while, I could visit him in a studio called Denham Studios outside London, which I loved, and saw him directing a movie. But I cannot remember the title of it. But we were very friendly, and I even-- we even exchanged letters which, like everything else, unfortunately, most of it was destroyed during the blitz. And then in 1943, all of a sudden, I heard the-- saw the terrible news that he had been shot down by a Messerschmitt over the Gulf of Biscay and after he came back from a film festival in Lisbon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happens then to your schooling? Now, please, I'd like to bring you back--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --into September.

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as my schooling was concerned, I went to school in Dorset in Bryanston College until I think it was June or July of 1940. By that time, France had fallen. Belgium had fallen. And Holland. And of course, Norway and Denmark. And they realized that England would be next. And of course, that was Hitler's dream to conquer England. And he started bombing London to soften up the progress for his troops to invade England. And I had to leave Dorset because it was part of the coast, the southern coast. And the British were very much afraid of fifth columnists since quite a number of fifth columnists had helped the Nazis in France and Holland and Belgium. And they thought maybe we, too, were fifth columnists, forgetting that we were Jewish and forgetting that we were young at the time because there was another boy there my age too-- Lowenthal. And we had to leave Dorset in July of 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were considered a danger to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called-- they called us enemy aliens. We even had to wear-- to carry a-- a little document with us at all times, which actually called us enemy aliens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your picture on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My picture was-- which, of course, I don't have anymore. That was back in 1939 or 1940 that we got it. So that's in case anybody questions us, we could-- we always had to produce this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they take you as an enemy alien? Where did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as an enemy alien, I was taken to Worcestershire, which is in the Midlands. There, they-- we didn't-- they didn't have to worry. Near a place called Evesham, between Evesham and Worcestershire. I can't remember. It's a tiny little village. But there was a youth hostel. And there must have been about 20 or 30 boys like me in there, my age and a little bit younger. And that's where we were supposed to stay, for us they say, for the duration of the war. And of course, we didn't realize what was going to happen. And we were taught carpentry by an Austrian refugee. And finally, we moved to Wales, partly because it became too expensive to stay there. We moved to Wales to cheaper accommodation and continued learning carpentry after we had converted a horse stable into a carpentry shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the place in Wales?

speaker: interviewee
text: The place was-- was called Tyn-y-coe. That was the name of the place. It was near a town called Brecknock in the Beacons, which are the highest mountains, as far as I remember, in Wales except for the Snowdon mountain. And it was a lovely time except for an Austrian beekeeper called Hecht who couldn't stand us-- couldn't stand boys. And we're staying at this particular place. And he always enjoyed when we were bitten by bees-- stung by bees, rather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he an Austrian Austrian?

speaker: interviewee
text: An Austria-- yeah, but he-- he was an Austrian Jew too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he was a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely. He just couldn't stand-- he had this whole place to himself before. And now, all of a sudden, he was invaded by about 30 of these youngsters. And he was an elderly man in his 60s already. So he was in his set ways and didn't enjoy our company.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Wales then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wales we stayed for about a year. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing? Just carpentry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Carpentry and gardening because we were supposed to grow vegetables for us and fruit. And that's what we did for most of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the carpentry productive? Were you making, actually producing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in fact, there's still one right there on the TV set. You can see a little frame which I made there. My sisters is on that frame-- in that frame.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after a year in Wales, did you stay at any particular place in Wales aside you gave us this name of the city? Was that a special camp? Was that a camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once, I went to a place called Cardiff, but that was only for a weekend. But otherwise, we couldn't really travel around much because we were considered enemy aliens. And we could only travel within a radius, I think, of 25 miles.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went up in with-- but we didn't mind it too much because we didn't have to worry about bombs there. And it was a peaceful time. And we had plenty of fun there. And after a year in Wales, where do you go then?

speaker: interviewee
text: After a year in Wales, we went to London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still then enemy alien?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we were enemy aliens. As far as I remember, we were always enemy aliens because as long as the war lasted, we didn't-- couldn't-- we were actually stateless of course. We lost our nationality the minute that we left Germany. But we were always considered enemy aliens by the British. But it was never in a-- a hostile way. It was just our title, so to speak, in case somebody asked us for our card. It says 'Enemy Alien' on there and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, but weren't you kept-- as an enemy alien, weren't you kept in special places that you had to stay initially?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- what-- that's why we did the youth hostel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in other places as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not while I was in the hostel.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, so then you say that-- so name, again, the places where you were interned when they interned you as enemy alien.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they interned-- that's-- the internment as such took place in 1940. That is right after France fell in July. All of a sudden, we had no idea what was going to happen to us while we were in one of these youth hostels in the Midlands in Evesham, outside Worcestershire. We were-- our entire building, our entire hostel, was surrounded by Tommies, by British soldiers. We had no idea why. And then we were told since we were enemy aliens, we had to be interned because England would be next on Hitler's list. And they did not want us to be-- to fight for Hitler's fifth columnists. And the fact, of course, that we couldn't because of our religion that were actually expelled from Nazi Germany made no difference to them because they couldn't trust us. Maybe we were fifth columnists after all. But then finally, we were taken to a number of internment camps. One of these camps was in Yorkshire. It was a pretty nice camp. It was racecourse. It used to be a racecourse. And they had converted it somehow, even though we still had to sleep on the straw that the horses also used. But we cleaned it up considerably and stayed there. Then, the next camp after-- we stayed there for about four or five weeks or so. We were taken to a camp in Lancashire called Warth Mill which used to be a run-down, totally obsolete, filthy textile factory. You could still see these huge-- the huge machinery stuck to the wall with clods of wool hanging from it-- filthy wool. And rats running around, and mice running around. And we were all given our sleeping bags and had to sleep on the concrete floor with the mice running around us. And finally, we got hold of some cheese which they had poisoned. And a number of them died. But we heard the screams of the mice and the rats during the night when they were-- they were-- when they were dying. And the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] something that I remember-- yes. In Warth Mill in this particular factory, there was one section for some reason, they had-- before we actually were sent to Warth Mill, they had just captured a German U-boat crew. And this U-boat crew-- before they actually transported them to a prisoner of war camp-- was also stationed at-- in Warth Mill. They had their own corner. And when we arrived, we noticed that somebody had painted a long, chalk, colored square. We were not supposed to cross this square because this was the domain of the Nazi U-boat crew. And when they heard that we were all Jewish refugees, they said, Judenschweine. But they couldn't do anything to us. They were just standing there screaming at us. They said, soon-- I mean, in German of course-- soon Hitler will be here, and then it will be like this for you. And we screamed back, the Nazi swine, et cetera, et cetera. And then one--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, was-- was there-- excuse me for interrupting-- was there anybody guarding the u-boat men and listening to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, absolutely. Nobody was guarding. The only thing is that some British soldiers were stationed outside this Warth Mill factory so that none of us could escape. But what happens inside was of no concern of theirs. And I can tell you the right of way, nothing ever happened. There was no battle, no fight but-- except for the insults that we screamed at each other. But one night-- we were not supposed to go to the bathroom at night because the bathroom was outside the actual building. And they were afraid if we went outside, we would somehow flash our flashlights, which we didn't have, and code some Morse code to the Luftwaffe planes. But in any case, so we were stationed there. And one night I had to go to the bathroom. Now, they had huge buckets in the hall, but they were overflowing. We couldn't use them anymore. So I said, to hell with it, I will go outside in any case. And as I approached the exit, I noticed one of the German U-boat marines was standing there too. And he asked me what I wanted. And I said, I've got to go to the bathroom, and I don't want to use the bucket. He said, you know, I feel the same way. I have to go to the bathroom too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, how can we do it because it was about 50 yards away. There was a distance of 50 yards between the bathroom and the factory. So he said, the only thing is we can make a charge for it. He said, I hope they won't shoot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happens he said, OK, give me your hand, and we run together. So it was a Jew and a Nazi U-boat guy holding hands, running through the night for the bathroom. Nothing happened. We did our business. And then we did it again. We rushed back, but this time there were shots. But they missed us because it was pitch black and because it was really the middle of the night, maybe 2:00 or 3:00 in the morning. But they missed us. And he was a real, sort of, blond Nazi guy in his 20s. And the next morning, I met him again. And he came to me. He stepped over his chalk side and shook my hands. And he said, listen, we are all the same. That's all he said. Nothing else. And then he walked back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guys at the-- the rest of the crew really gave him hell apparently. I mean, you could see them shouting at him and saying, why do you mix with Jews and so on?

speaker: interviewee
text: But in any case, that was my experience in Warth Mill. Then we went to another camp. I cannot remember it. But finally, to the Isle of Man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in the Isle of Man?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the Isle of Man, about two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that difficult there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a wonderful life. We had a wonderful life. And the British captain in charge of our camp was the brother of Anthony Eden.
